Iteration-dependent analysis

class autolyap.IterationDependent[source]

Bases: object

Iteration-dependent Lyapunov analysis utilities.

Class-level reference

This class-level docstring centralizes notation shared across methods. Method-level docstrings focus on API details: inputs, outputs, and validation.

Problem and algorithm context

Notation follows InclusionProblem and Algorithm. The sets \(\IndexFunc\), \(\IndexOp\) and counts \(\NumEval\), \(\NumEvalFunc\), \(\NumFunc\) are inherited from those classes. We consider sequences

\[(\bx^{k},\bu^{k},\by^{k},\bFcn^{k})_{k\in\naturals}\]

generated by the state-space representation, together with a solution triplet \((y^{\star},\hat{\bu}^{\star},\bFcn^{\star})\).

Star variables

A solution triplet means there exists a vector

\[\bu^{\star} = (u_1^{\star},\ldots,u_m^{\star}),\]

such that

\[\forall i \in \IndexFunc, \quad u_i^{\star}\in\partial f_i(y^{\star}), \qquad \forall i \in \IndexOp, \quad u_i^{\star}\in G_i(y^{\star}), \qquad \sum_{i=1}^{m} u_i^{\star}=0.\]

Hence, each \(u_i^{\star}\) is the component output at \(y^{\star}\).

The reduced vector \(\hat{\bu}^{\star}\) collects the first \(m-1\) components of \(\bu^{\star}\):

\[\hat{\bu}^{\star} = (u_1^{\star},\ldots,u_{m-1}^{\star}), \qquad u_m^{\star} = -\sum_{i=1}^{m-1} u_i^{\star}.\]

The function-value vector is

\[\bFcn^{\star}=(f_i(y^{\star}))_{i\in\IndexFunc}.\]

Lyapunov ansatz

Fix a horizon \(K \in \naturals\). For each \(k \in \llbracket 0, K \rrbracket\), define

\[\begin{split}\boldsymbol{z}_{\mathcal{V}}^{k} = \begin{bmatrix} \bx^{k} \\ \bu^{k} \\ \hat{\bu}^{\star} \\ y^{\star} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \boldsymbol{f}_{\mathcal{V}}^{k} = \begin{bmatrix} \bFcn^{k} \\ \bFcn^{\star} \end{bmatrix},\end{split}\]
\[\mathcal{V}(k) = \inner{ \boldsymbol{z}_{\mathcal{V}}^{k} }{ (Q_k \kron \Id)\boldsymbol{z}_{\mathcal{V}}^{k} } + q_{k}^{\top}\boldsymbol{f}_{\mathcal{V}}^{k}.\]

Dimensions

\[Q_k \in \sym^{n + \NumEval + m}, \qquad q_k \in \mathbb{R}^{\NumEvalFunc + \NumFunc}.\]

Here \(n\) is the state dimension (as in Algorithm). The vector \(\hat{\bu}^{\star}\) is void when \(m = 1\). When \(\NumFunc = 0\), the vectors \(q_k\) are omitted.

Chained Lyapunov inequalities

We set the endpoint parameters \((Q_0,q_0)\) and \((Q_K,q_K)\) and search for intermediate \((Q_k,q_k)\) for \(k \in \llbracket 1, K-1 \rrbracket\), together with a scalar \(c \ge 0\), such that

\[\mathcal{V}(K) \le \mathcal{V}(K-1) \le \cdots \le \mathcal{V}(1) \le c\,\mathcal{V}(0).\]

Role of K

  • \(K\) is the iteration budget and final index in the chained inequalities.

  • The chain links the Lyapunov values from \(k=0\) to \(k=K\).

Convergence conclusions

When the chained inequalities are feasible, there exists \(c \ge 0\) such that

\[\mathcal{V}(K) \le c\,\mathcal{V}(0).\]
static get_parameters_distance_to_solution(
algo: Type[Algorithm],
k: int,
i: int = 1,
j: int = 1,
) → ndarray | Tuple[ndarray, ndarray][source]

Compute Lyapunov parameters for the distance-to-solution metric at iteration \(k\).

Mathematical notation and shared definitions follow the class-level reference in IterationDependent.

This method computes

\[Q_k = \left(P_{(i,j)}Y_k^{k,k} - P_{(i,\star)}Y_\star^{k,k}\right)^\top \left(P_{(i,j)}Y_k^{k,k} - P_{(i,\star)}Y_\star^{k,k}\right),\]

where \(Y_k^{k,k}\) and \(Y_\star^{k,k}\) come from get_Ys() with k_min = k, k_max = k, and \(P_{(i,j)}\) and \(P_{(i,\star)}\) come from get_Ps().

If algo.m_func > 0, the method also returns \(q_k = 0\).

Resulting lower bound

With this choice of \((Q_k,q_k)\),

\[\mathcal{V}(k) = \|y_{i,j}^{k} - y^{\star}\|^{2}.\]

Parameters

  • algo (Type[Algorithm]): Algorithm instance providing m, m_bar_is, get_Ys(), and get_Ps().

  • k (int): Nonnegative iteration index \(k\).

  • i (int): Component index, with \(i \in \llbracket 1, m\rrbracket\).

  • j (int): Evaluation index for component i, with \(j \in \llbracket 1, \NumEval_i\rrbracket\).

Returns

  • (Union[numpy.ndarray, Tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]]): If algo.m_func == 0, returns \(Q_k\) with

    \[Q_k \in \sym^{n + \NumEval + m}.\]

    Otherwise, returns \((Q_k, q_k)\) with

    \[\begin{split}\begin{aligned} Q_k &\in \sym^{n + \NumEval + m},\\ q_k &\in \mathbb{R}^{\NumEvalFunc + \NumFunc}. \end{aligned}\end{split}\]

Raises

  • ValueError: If an input is invalid or a required matrix is missing.

static get_parameters_fixed_point_residual(
algo: Type[Algorithm],
k: int,
) → ndarray | Tuple[ndarray, ndarray][source]

Compute Lyapunov parameters for the fixed-point residual at iteration \(k\).

Mathematical notation and shared definitions follow the class-level reference in IterationDependent.

This method computes

\[Q_k = \left(X_{k+1}^{k,k} - X_k^{k,k}\right)^\top \left(X_{k+1}^{k,k} - X_k^{k,k}\right),\]

using \(X_k^{k,k}\) and \(X_{k+1}^{k,k}\) from get_Xs() with k_min = k, k_max = k.

If algo.m_func > 0, the method also returns \(q_k = 0\).

Resulting lower bound

With this choice of \((Q_k,q_k)\),

\[\mathcal{V}(k) = \|\bx^{k+1} - \bx^{k}\|^{2}.\]

Parameters

  • algo (Type[Algorithm]): Algorithm instance providing dimensions and get_Xs().

  • k (int): Nonnegative iteration index \(k\).

Returns

  • (Union[numpy.ndarray, Tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]]): If algo.m_func == 0, returns \(Q_k\) with

    \[Q_k \in \sym^{n + \NumEval + m}.\]

    Otherwise, returns \((Q_k, q_k)\) with

    \[\begin{split}\begin{aligned} Q_k &\in \sym^{n + \NumEval + m},\\ q_k &\in \mathbb{R}^{\NumEvalFunc + \NumFunc}. \end{aligned}\end{split}\]

Raises

  • ValueError: If an input is invalid or required \(X\) matrices are missing.

static get_parameters_function_value_suboptimality(
algo: Type[Algorithm],
k: int,
j: int = 1,
) → Tuple[ndarray, ndarray][source]

Compute Lyapunov parameters for function-value suboptimality at iteration \(k\).

Mathematical notation and shared definitions follow the class-level reference in IterationDependent. This method is defined only when \(m = \NumFunc = 1\).

The returned parameters satisfy

\[Q_k = 0, \qquad q_k = \left(F_{(1,j,k)}^{k,k} - F_{(1,\star,\star)}^{k,k}\right)^\top,\]

where both \(F\) matrices are retrieved from get_Fs() with k_min = k, k_max = k.

Resulting lower bound

With this choice of \((Q_k,q_k)\),

\[\mathcal{V}(k) = f_{1}(y_{1,j}^{k}) - f_{1}(y^{\star}).\]

Parameters

  • algo (Type[Algorithm]): Algorithm instance with m = m_func = 1, m_bar_is, and get_Fs().

  • k (int): Nonnegative iteration index \(k\).

  • j (int): Evaluation index for component 1, with \(j \in \llbracket 1, \NumEval_1\rrbracket\).

Returns

  • (Tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]): A tuple \((Q_k, q_k)\) with

    \[\begin{split}\begin{aligned} Q_k &\in \sym^{n + \NumEval + m},\\ q_k &\in \mathbb{R}^{\NumEvalFunc + \NumFunc}. \end{aligned}\end{split}\]

Raises

  • ValueError: If an input is invalid, required \(F\) matrices are missing, or \(m \ne 1\) / \(\NumFunc \ne 1\).

static get_parameters_optimality_measure(
algo: Type[Algorithm],
k: int,
) → ndarray | Tuple[ndarray, ndarray][source]

Compute Lyapunov parameters for the optimality measure at iteration \(k\).

Mathematical notation and shared definitions follow the class-level reference in IterationDependent.

This method computes

\[\begin{split}Q_k = \begin{cases} \left(P_{(1,1)}U_k^{k,k}\right)^\top \left(P_{(1,1)}U_k^{k,k}\right), & \text{if } m = 1, \\[1em] \left(\sum_{i=1}^{m} P_{(i,1)}U_k^{k,k}\right)^\top \left(\sum_{i=1}^{m} P_{(i,1)}U_k^{k,k}\right) + \sum_{i=2}^{m} \left(\left(P_{(1,1)} - P_{(i,1)}\right)Y_k^{k,k}\right)^\top \left(\left(P_{(1,1)} - P_{(i,1)}\right)Y_k^{k,k}\right), & \text{if } m > 1, \end{cases}\end{split}\]

using \(U_k^{k,k}\), \(Y_k^{k,k}\), and projection matrices from get_Us(), get_Ys(), and get_Ps().

If algo.m_func > 0, the method also returns \(q_k = 0\).

Resulting lower bound

With this choice of \((Q_k,q_k)\),

\[\begin{split}\mathcal{V}(k) = \begin{cases} \|u_{1,1}^{k}\|^{2}, & \text{if } m = 1, \\[0.5em] \left\|\sum_{i=1}^{m} u_{i,1}^{k}\right\|^{2} + \sum_{i=2}^{m} \|y_{1,1}^{k} - y_{i,1}^{k}\|^{2}, & \text{if } m > 1. \end{cases}\end{split}\]

Parameters

  • algo (Type[Algorithm]): Algorithm instance providing m, m_bar, m_bar_is, get_Us(), get_Ys(), and get_Ps().

  • k (int): Nonnegative iteration index \(k\).

Returns

  • (Union[numpy.ndarray, Tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]]): If algo.m_func == 0, returns \(Q_k\) with

    \[Q_k \in \sym^{n + \NumEval + m}.\]

    Otherwise, returns \((Q_k, q_k)\) with

    \[\begin{split}\begin{aligned} Q_k &\in \sym^{n + \NumEval + m},\\ q_k &\in \mathbb{R}^{\NumEvalFunc + \NumFunc}. \end{aligned}\end{split}\]

Raises

  • ValueError: If an input is invalid or required matrices are missing.

static verify_iteration_dependent_Lyapunov(
prob: Type[InclusionProblem],
algo: Type[Algorithm],
K: int,
Q_0: ndarray,
Q_K: ndarray,
q_0: ndarray | None = None,
q_K: ndarray | None = None,
solver_options: SolverOptions | None = None,
) → Dict[str, object][source]

Verify feasibility of iteration-dependent Lyapunov inequalities via an SDP.

This method formulates and solves a semidefinite program (SDP) for a given inclusion problem, algorithm, and endpoint targets \((Q_0,q_0)\) and \((Q_K,q_K)\).

Mathematical notation, star-variable definitions, and Lyapunov ansatzes follow the class-level reference in IterationDependent.

Enforced inequalities

The method enforces the chained Lyapunov inequalities

\[\mathcal{V}(K) \le \mathcal{V}(K-1) \le \cdots \le \mathcal{V}(1) \le c\,\mathcal{V}(0).\]

with \(c \ge 0\).

User-specified targets

The user sets the endpoint parameters \((Q_0,q_0)\) and \((Q_K,q_K)\). These endpoint parameters are fixed inputs to the SDP (not optimization variables). The SDP then searches for intermediate \((Q_k,q_k)\) for \(k \in \llbracket 1, K-1\rrbracket\) and the minimum feasible \(c\). When \(\NumFunc = 0\), the vectors \(q_k\) (and inputs q_0, q_K) are omitted.

The Lyapunov values are

\[\mathcal{V}(k) = \inner{ \boldsymbol{z}_{\mathcal{V}}^{k} }{ (Q_k \kron \Id)\boldsymbol{z}_{\mathcal{V}}^{k} } + q_{k}^{\top}\boldsymbol{f}_{\mathcal{V}}^{k},\]

where

\[\begin{split}\boldsymbol{z}_{\mathcal{V}}^{k} = \begin{bmatrix} \bx^{k} \\ \bu^{k} \\ \hat{\bu}^{\star} \\ y^{\star} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \boldsymbol{f}_{\mathcal{V}}^{k} = \begin{bmatrix} \bFcn^{k} \\ \bFcn^{\star} \end{bmatrix}.\end{split}\]

Parameters

  • prob (Type[InclusionProblem]): An InclusionProblem instance containing interpolation conditions.

  • algo (Type[Algorithm]): An Algorithm instance providing dimensions and matrix helpers.

  • K (int): A positive integer corresponding to \(K\) defining the iteration budget.

  • Q_0 (numpy.ndarray): A symmetric matrix corresponding to \(Q_0 \in \sym^{n + \NumEval + m}\).

  • Q_K (numpy.ndarray): A symmetric matrix corresponding to \(Q_K \in \sym^{n + \NumEval + m}\).

  • q_0 (Optional[numpy.ndarray]): A vector corresponding to \(q_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{\NumEvalFunc + \NumFunc}\) for functional components (required if \(\NumFunc > 0\); otherwise None).

  • q_K (Optional[numpy.ndarray]): A vector corresponding to \(q_K \in \mathbb{R}^{\NumEvalFunc + \NumFunc}\) for functional components (required if \(\NumFunc > 0\); otherwise None).

  • solver_options (Optional[SolverOptions]): Optional backend and parameter settings. Defaults to SolverOptions(backend=”mosek_fusion”).

Returns

  • (Dict[str, object]): Result dictionary with keys success, c, and certificate.

    • success (bool): True iff the SDP is feasible.

    • c (Optional[float]): Optimal objective value when success is True; otherwise None.

    • certificate (Optional[Dict[str, object]]): None when success is False; otherwise a feasible certificate.

    When success is True, certificate has:

    {
  "Q_sequence": List[np.ndarray],   # length K+1, Q_sequence[k] = Q_k
  "q_sequence": List[np.ndarray] | None,  # None when m_func == 0
  "multipliers": {
    "operator_lambda": List[record],
    "function_lambda": List[record],
    "function_nu": List[record]
  }
}

    Each multiplier list entry is a record with:

    record = {
  "iteration": int,
  "component": int,
  "interpolation_index": int,
  "pairs": List[{"j": int | "star", "k": int | "star"}],
  "value": float
}

    Field meanings and ranges:

    • iteration corresponds to \(k\) and satisfies \(k \in \llbracket 0, K-1 \rrbracket\).

    • component corresponds to \(i\) and satisfies \(i \in \llbracket 1, m \rrbracket\).

    • interpolation_index corresponds to \(o\), where \(o\) is the zero-based index from \(\text{enumerate}(\text{prob.get_component_data}(i))\), so \(o \in \llbracket 0, \text{len}(\text{prob.get_component_data}(i)) - 1 \rrbracket\).

    • pairs is the concrete interpolation-pair list used by that multiplier. Its length is 1 (pattern “r1”) or 2 (patterns “r1<r2”, “r1!=r2”, “r1!=star”). Typical examples are [{“j”: 2, “k”: 5}] and [{“j”: 1, “k”: 5}, {“j”: “star”, “k”: “star”}].

      Pair entries satisfy

      \[\begin{split}\begin{aligned} j &\in \llbracket 1, \bar m_i \rrbracket \cup \{\star\}, \\ k_{\text{pair}} &\in \{k, k+1\} \cup \{\star\}, \end{aligned}\end{split}\]

      with \(j=\star \Leftrightarrow k_{\text{pair}}=\star\).

    • value is the scalar multiplier for that record.

Raises

  • ValueError: If input dimensions or other conditions are violated.

Notes

  • Default backend is MOSEK Fusion. You may also select CVXPY via SolverOptions(backend=”cvxpy”).
